name: Chicken Enchiladas
description: Corn tortillas filled with shredded chicken and cheese, baked in red enchilada sauce.
instructions: Poach chicken in salted water until cooked; shred with two forks.
Sauté diced onion and garlic in oil, add shredded chicken, cumin, and salt.
Spread a thin layer of enchilada sauce on the bottom of a baking dish.
Fill each tortilla with chicken mixture and cheese, roll up and place seam-down.
Pour remaining sauce over the enchiladas, top with remaining cheese.
Bake at 190C for 20 minutes until bubbly. Top with sour cream and cilantro.
tips: Briefly warm tortillas in oil before rolling to prevent cracking.
Use a mix of cheddar and Monterey Jack for the best melt.

name: Carnitas
description: Slow-braised pork shoulder shredded and crisped, served in tacos with onion and cilantro.
instructions: Cut pork into large chunks, season with salt, pepper, cumin, and oregano.
Place in Dutch oven with orange juice, lime juice, garlic, and quartered onion.
Cover and braise at 160C for 2.5 hours until fork-tender.
Shred pork, spread on a baking sheet, and broil 5 minutes until edges are crispy.
Warm tortillas and fill with carnitas.
Top with diced white onion, cilantro, and a squeeze of lime.
tips: The orange juice tenderizes the pork and adds subtle sweetness.
Broiling the shredded meat at the end creates the signature crispy edges.

name: Tacos al Pastor
description: Marinated pork tacos with pineapple, onion, and cilantro on corn tortillas.
instructions: Soak dried chilies in hot water 15 minutes. Blend with achiote, garlic, vinegar, cumin, and oregano.
Slice pork thinly, marinate in chili sauce for at least 2 hours.
Grill pork slices on high heat until charred, about 3 minutes per side.
Grill pineapple slices until caramelized.
Chop pork and pineapple into small pieces.
Serve in warm corn tortillas with diced onion, cilantro, and lime.
tips: The chili-achiote marinade is what gives al pastor its signature red color.
Charring the pineapple brings out its sweetness.

name: Chiles Rellenos
description: Roasted poblano peppers stuffed with cheese, battered, and fried in a tomato sauce.
instructions: Roast poblanos over an open flame or under broiler until charred. Place in a bag to steam 10 minutes, then peel.
Make a small slit in each pepper and remove seeds. Stuff with cheese.
Separate eggs. Beat whites to stiff peaks, fold in yolks and flour.
Dip stuffed peppers in batter, fry in oil until golden on all sides.
Blend tomatoes with onion, garlic, and cumin for the sauce. Simmer 10 minutes.
Serve peppers in warm tomato sauce.
tips: The egg batter should be fluffy like a cloud — stiff egg whites are essential.
Do not remove the stem — it helps keep the chile intact during frying.

name: Pozole Rojo
description: Traditional Mexican hominy soup with pork in a red chili broth, garnished with cabbage, radish, and lime.
instructions: Simmer pork shoulder in salted water with quartered onion and garlic for 1.5 hours until tender. Shred.
Toast dried chilies in a dry pan, soak in hot water 15 minutes, then blend with garlic and cumin.
Strain chili sauce into the pork broth. Add drained hominy and oregano.
Simmer 20 minutes for flavors to meld.
Serve in deep bowls with shredded cabbage, sliced radishes, lime, and oregano.
Accompany with tostadas on the side.
tips: Toast the chilies just until fragrant — burnt chilies make a bitter sauce.
Pozole always improves the next day.

name: Chicken Mole
description: Chicken braised in a complex, rich mole sauce with chocolate, dried chilies, and spices.
instructions: Toast dried chilies in a dry pan, soak in hot water 20 minutes.
Toast sesame seeds, almonds, and spices in a dry pan until fragrant.
Blend soaked chilies, toasted nuts and seeds, onion, garlic, tomatoes, raisins, cumin, and cinnamon into a smooth paste.
Fry the paste in oil for 5 minutes, stirring constantly.
Add chicken stock and chocolate, stir until melted and combined. Simmer 10 minutes.
Add chicken pieces, cover and braise 40 minutes until chicken is fall-off-the-bone tender. Serve with rice.
tips: Mole has 20+ ingredients — the complexity is the point. Do not simplify.
The chocolate adds richness, not sweetness — use dark, bitter Mexican chocolate.

name: Quesadillas
description: Crispy flour tortillas filled with melted cheese, peppers, onions, and chicken.
instructions: Sauté diced bell pepper, onion, and jalapeño until soft.
Place a tortilla in a dry pan, layer cheese, chicken, and sautéed vegetables.
Top with more cheese and a second tortilla.
Cook on medium heat 2-3 minutes per side until golden and crispy.
Cut into wedges and serve with sour cream and salsa.
tips: Medium heat gives cheese time to melt before the tortilla burns.
Press down gently while cooking for even crisping.

name: Burrito Bowl
description: Deconstructed burrito with cilantro lime rice, black beans, corn, salsa, guacamole, and choice of protein.
instructions: Season chicken with cumin, chili powder, and salt; grill or pan-fry until cooked. Slice.
Mix rice with lime juice and chopped cilantro.
Warm black beans with a pinch of cumin.
Assemble bowls: cilantro-lime rice, black beans, corn, sliced chicken.
Top with sliced avocado, salsa, sour cream, and shredded lettuce.
Squeeze lime over everything and serve.
tips: Cilantro-lime rice is what makes this restaurant quality.
For meal prep, keep wet toppings separate until serving.

name: Mexican Street Corn (Elote)
description: Grilled corn on the cob slathered with mayo, cotija cheese, chili powder, and lime.
instructions: Grill corn over high heat, turning occasionally, until charred in spots (about 10 minutes).
Mix mayo with garlic powder.
Brush grilled corn generously with mayo mixture.
Sprinkle with crumbled cotija cheese and chili powder.
Squeeze lime juice over the corn and garnish with cilantro.
tips: Cotija cheese is salty — go easy on salt elsewhere.
If no grill, broil the corn in the oven for similar char.

name: Huevos Rancheros
description: Fried eggs on corn tortillas with ranchero sauce, black beans, and avocado.
instructions: Make ranchero sauce: sauté diced onion, garlic, and jalapeño in oil. Add tomatoes and cumin, simmer 10 minutes.
Warm black beans in a small pot with a pinch of cumin.
Fry eggs sunny-side-up in a separate pan.
Lightly fry tortillas in oil until just crispy at edges.
Place tortillas on plates, spread with beans, ladle ranchero sauce, top with fried eggs.
Garnish with sliced avocado and cilantro.
tips: The yolks should be runny — they mix with the sauce beautifully.
Cotija cheese crumbled on top is an excellent addition.

name: Chilaquiles
description: Crispy tortilla chips simmered in salsa verde, topped with crema, cheese, and a fried egg.
instructions: Heat salsa verde in a large skillet until simmering.
Add tortilla chips, toss to coat, cook 2-3 minutes until chips soften slightly but still have some crunch.
Fry eggs sunny-side-up in a separate pan.
Plate the chilaquiles, drizzle with crema.
Top with crumbled queso fresco, sliced avocado, sliced onion, and cilantro.
Crown with the fried eggs and serve immediately.
tips: Use thick, sturdy tortilla chips — thin ones disintegrate instantly.
Serve immediately — chilaquiles go from perfect to soggy fast.

name: Tamales
description: Corn masa dough filled with pork in red chili sauce, steamed in corn husks.
instructions: Soak corn husks in warm water for 1 hour. Simmer pork until tender, shred.
Toast and soak chilies, blend with garlic, cumin, and some pork broth. Simmer shredded pork in this sauce.
Beat lard until fluffy, gradually mix in masa harina, stock, baking powder, and salt until a spreadable dough forms.
Spread masa on corn husks, add a spoonful of pork filling, fold and tie.
Stand tamales upright in a steamer, steam for 1-1.5 hours until masa pulls away from husk.
Rest 10 minutes before unwrapping and serving with salsa.
tips: The masa is ready when a small ball floats in water.
Do not overfill — the masa needs room to expand during steaming.

name: Sopes
description: Thick corn masa bases with pinched edges, topped with beans, meat, lettuce, and crema.
instructions: Mix masa harina with water and salt to form a soft dough. Divide into 8 balls.
Flatten each into a thick disc (about 8cm across, 1cm thick).
Cook on a dry griddle for 2 minutes per side until cooked but not crispy.
While still warm, pinch edges up to form a shallow rim.
Lightly fry sopes in oil until golden on the bottom.
Top with warm refried beans, shredded chicken, lettuce, crema, queso fresco, and salsa.
tips: Pinch the edges while the masa is still warm — it hardens as it cools.
The base should be crispy outside but soft inside.

name: Tostadas
description: Crispy flat corn tortillas topped with refried beans, chicken, lettuce, avocado, and salsa.
instructions: Fry tortillas in oil until flat and crispy on both sides; drain on paper towels.
Spread warm refried beans on each tostada.
Layer with shredded chicken and shredded lettuce.
Top with sliced avocado, crumbled queso fresco, a drizzle of crema, and salsa.
Squeeze lime over the top and serve immediately.
tips: Eat tostadas from the edges inward — biting from the center makes everything fall off.
The base must be completely flat and crispy to support the toppings.

name: Carne Asada
description: Marinated grilled skirt steak sliced thin, served with warm tortillas, guacamole, and salsa.
instructions: Blend lime juice, orange juice, garlic, jalapeño, cilantro, cumin, oil, and salt into a marinade.
Marinate steak for at least 1 hour (up to overnight).
Grill over very high heat for 3-4 minutes per side for medium-rare.
Rest 5 minutes, then slice thinly against the grain.
Grill onion slices alongside the steak.
Serve with warm tortillas, grilled onions, guacamole, and salsa.
tips: Skirt steak must be cooked over very high heat for a charred crust.
Always slice against the grain for tender results.

name: Chicken Fajitas
description: Sizzling marinated chicken strips with peppers and onions, served with warm tortillas.
instructions: Slice chicken into strips, marinate with lime juice, cumin, chili powder, paprika, garlic, oil, and salt for 30 minutes.
Heat a cast iron pan until smoking. Cook chicken strips until charred, about 3 minutes per side; set aside.
In the same pan, stir-fry sliced peppers and onions until slightly charred but still crunchy.
Return chicken to the pan, toss together.
Serve sizzling on a hot plate with warm tortillas, sour cream, and guacamole.
tips: A screaming hot pan is essential for the signature fajita char.
Do not crowd the pan — cook in batches for proper searing.

name: Arroz con Pollo
description: One-pot Mexican chicken and rice cooked with tomatoes, peppers, and spices.
instructions: Season chicken with salt and cumin. Brown skin-side down in oil until golden; set aside.
Sauté diced onion, garlic, and bell pepper in the same pot for 3 minutes.
Add rice, stir 1 minute to toast. Add tomatoes, stock, and oregano.
Nestle chicken on top, bring to a boil, then cover and reduce to low.
Cook 20 minutes until rice is tender and chicken is cooked through.
Add peas in the last 5 minutes. Fluff rice and serve.
tips: Do not stir the rice after adding liquid — let it cook undisturbed.
The chicken drippings flavor the rice as it cooks.

name: Mexican Black Beans
description: Slow-simmered black beans with onion, garlic, cumin, and epazote.
instructions: Soak beans overnight in water. Drain and rinse.
Place beans in a pot with quartered onion, whole garlic cloves, jalapeño, and epazote.
Cover with water by 5cm, bring to a boil, then simmer 1-1.5 hours until creamy.
Add salt, cumin, and olive oil in the last 15 minutes of cooking.
Mash some beans against the side of the pot for a creamier texture.
Serve garnished with cilantro and a squeeze of lime.
tips: Do not add salt until the beans are nearly done — salt toughens the skins early on.
Epazote is the traditional Mexican herb for beans — oregano is a good substitute.

name: Guacamole with Chips
description: Chunky fresh guacamole with ripe avocados, lime, cilantro, onion, and jalapeño, served with tortilla chips.
instructions: Halve avocados and scoop flesh into a bowl. Mash with a fork to desired chunkiness.
Fold in finely diced onion, jalapeño (seeds removed for less heat), and diced tomato.
Add lime juice, chopped cilantro, and salt. Stir gently.
Taste and adjust salt and lime.
Serve immediately with tortilla chips.
tips: Leave some chunks — guacamole should not be a smooth paste.
Press plastic wrap directly onto the surface to prevent browning if storing.

name: Churros
description: Crispy fried dough sticks coated in cinnamon sugar, served with chocolate dipping sauce.
instructions: Bring water, butter, 1 tbsp sugar, and salt to a boil. Remove from heat, stir in flour until a smooth dough forms.
Cool slightly, beat in egg and vanilla until smooth and pipeable.
Heat oil to 180C. Pipe dough through a star-tipped piping bag directly into oil (cut with scissors).
Fry 3-4 minutes, turning, until deep golden and crispy. Drain on paper towels.
Roll hot churros in cinnamon-sugar mixture (remaining sugar + cinnamon).
Make chocolate sauce: melt chocolate with cream. Serve churros with chocolate for dipping.
tips: The star tip is essential — it creates ridges that crisp up beautifully.
Fry at 180C exactly — too low and they absorb oil, too high and they burn outside before cooking inside.
